user_request: Draft a suit for recovery of Rs.20,00,000/- paid as advance for a business transaction which failed due to Defendant's default. Plead total failure of consideration under Section 65 of Indian Contract Act. Claim refund with interest and costs. Include cause of action paragraph with accrual date, continuing nature of cause of action, valuation, court fee, and proper verification clause. Draft suitable for filing before the District Court.

facts.summary: Plaintiff seeks recovery of Rs. 20,00,000 paid as advance for a business transaction that failed due to the Defendant's default. The suit claims total failure of consideration under Section 65 of the Indian Contract Act, seeking refund with interest and costs.

assumptions: Jurisdiction lies in the specified District Court based on transaction location or defendant residence
Suit is within limitation period from date of knowledge of failure

In case where the subject matter is not capable of being estimated at a money value, it is provided by s 9 of the 
Suits Valuation Act, 1887, that the value of the suit for purposes of jurisdiction is what the High Court may 
specify by rules made under that section. Where no rules are made, the High Courts of Allahabad and Calcutta 
have said that the safest and most convenient course is to treat the valuation made by the plaintiff as prima 
facie the true valuation, but subject to correction by the court. If the defective valuation has been due to an 
improper motive, the court must decide what should be considered to be the proper value. 53 The cases in which 
the above rule was laid down were suits for restitution of conjugal rights. In a subsequent case, however, the

appropriate in suits for restitution of conjugal rights, it was not so in suits which affected property, and that the 
best rule in such cases was to value the suits according to the value of the property liable to be affected 
thereby.54 Thus, a suit to compel registration of an instrument whether the instrument be registrable

jurisdictional purposes should be on the basis of annual rent or profit. Suit so valued along with the claim of 
damage exceeding pecuniary jurisdiction of the court is liable to be returned for prosecution before the proper

sections.body:
SUIT FOR RECOVERY OF Rs. {{CLAIM_AMOUNT}}/- (Rupees {{CLAIM_AMOUNT_WORDS}} Only) WITH INTEREST AND COSTS
(Filed under Section 26 of the Code of Civil Procedure, 1908)

sections.heading: BETWEEN
sections.instruction: Draft the parties section listing plaintiff and defendant(s) with full particulars: name, age, occupation, and address. Use sui juris averment for competency. If any detail is missing, use the appropriate {{PLACEHOLDER}}.

sections.heading: JURISDICTION
sections.instruction: Draft the jurisdiction section establishing territorial and pecuniary jurisdiction. For territorial jurisdiction, state SPECIFIC facts: where the Defendant resides or carries on business, where payment was made, where the agreement was executed, or where the cause of action arose — with concrete place references. For pecuniary jurisdiction, state the claim amount and that it falls within this court's monetary limits.

examples.bad: The cause of action arose within the jurisdiction of this Court.
examples.good: The Defendant resides and carries on business at {{DEFENDANT_ADDRESS}}, which falls within the territorial jurisdiction of this Court. Further, the transaction was executed within the local limits of this Court's jurisdiction.

sections.heading: FACTS OF THE CASE
sections.instruction: Draft the facts section as numbered paragraphs in chronological narrative form. Each paragraph must tell a story — not state conclusions. Cover: (1) How the parties came to know each other and the nature of their relationship, (2) What was agreed — the specific terms of the transaction/agreement, (3) What obligations each party undertook and the timeline for performance, (4) The specific transaction — payment made, goods/services supplied, invoice raised, loan given, etc. with amount, mode, date, (5) What exactly constituted the defendant's failure — describe the specific default with detail, (6) What happened after the default — communications, demands, legal notice and response. Reference documents as 'Annexure [LABEL]'. Do NOT state legal conclusions in this section — keep it purely factual.

examples.good: Despite repeated demands, the Defendant neither performed his obligations under the agreement nor paid the amount of Rs. {{CLAIM_AMOUNT}}/- to the Plaintiff. The Defendant's last communication was on {{DATE}}, wherein he sought further time but made no payment whatsoever.

sections.heading: LEGAL BASIS AND GROUNDS
sections.instruction: Draft the legal basis section in paragraph form. Choose the statutory provisions that BEST FIT the specific facts of this money recovery — the legal theory must match the nature of the transaction (e.g., breach of contract, goods sold and delivered, loan recovery, restitution, etc.). For each provision: (1) State what the provision says — the legal rule it creates, (2) Explain what factual conditions trigger its application, (3) Show how the facts of this case satisfy those conditions. If applicable, include an alternative plea using 'In the alternative and without prejudice to the above...' framing. Include defensive pleading where appropriate. Cite provisions from the VERIFIED STATUTORY PROVISIONS list in context.

examples.bad: Under Section X, the Defendant is bound to pay.
examples.good: [Applicable Section] of the [Applicable Act] provides that [state the legal rule]. The conditions for invoking this provision are [state conditions]. In the present case, [show how the specific facts — the nature of the transaction, the obligation, and the default — satisfy those conditions].

must_include.description: At least one statutory section cited

sections.heading: CAUSE OF ACTION
sections.instruction: Draft the cause of action section in PARAGRAPH prose (not numbered sub-headings). Must cover: (1) ORIGIN — when and how the right to sue first arose, anchored to the default/failure date, (2) FURTHER ACCRUAL — when the cause of action further arose (e.g., failure to comply with legal notice), (3) CONTINUITY — that the cause of action is continuing because the defendant continues to wrongfully retain the money. The cause must be anchored to the failure/default date, NOT the notice date. Write in flowing paragraph form — avoid mechanical sub-headings like '(a) underlying obligation'.

examples.bad: Cause of action arose on X date.
examples.good: The cause of action for the present suit first arose on {{DEFAULT_DATE}} when the Defendant failed to perform his obligations despite being bound to do so, and the Plaintiff's right to claim the amount thereby accrued. The cause of action further and continuously arose on {{NOTICE_DATE}} when the Defendant, despite receipt of the legal notice demanding payment, failed, neglected, and refused to pay the said amount.

sections.heading: LIMITATION
sections.instruction: Draft the limitation section stating: (1) the applicable Article of the Limitation Act, 1963 Schedule (from enrichment or use placeholder), (2) the limitation period, (3) the starting date of limitation (must be anchored to cause_of_action_date/default_date, NOT notice_date), (4) that the suit is within time. If limitation article is not available in enrichment, use {{LIMITATION_ARTICLE}} placeholder.

sections.heading: VALUATION AND COURT FEE
sections.instruction: Draft the valuation and court fee section stating: (1) suit valuation equals the claim amount, (2) court fee calculated per applicable state court fees act. Use court_fee context from web search if available, otherwise use {{COURT_FEE_AMOUNT}} placeholder.

sections.heading: INTEREST
sections.instruction: Draft the interest section with a 3-part structure: (1) Pre-suit interest — from default date to filing date with rate and justification, (2) Pendente lite interest — during pendency of suit under Section 34 CPC with rate, (3) Future interest — post-decree until realization with rate. Justify the interest rate: state why the claimed rate is appropriate (commercial transaction, wrongful retention, prevailing bank rate, contractual understanding). Cite Section 34 of CPC for pendente lite interest.

sections.heading: PRAYER
sections.instruction: Draft the prayer section with at least 6 specific sub-prayers as lettered items (a, b, c, ...). Each prayer must state exact amounts or rate placeholders — never vague. Must include: (a) decree for principal amount with Rs. figure, (b) pre-suit interest with rate and date range, (c) pendente lite interest under Section 34 CPC with rate, (d) future interest post-decree with rate until realization, (e) costs of the suit including pre-litigation costs for sending legal notice, (f) any other relief the court deems fit and proper in the facts and circumstances.

sections.heading: LIST OF DOCUMENTS RELIED UPON
sections.instruction: Generate a numbered list of documents the plaintiff relies upon, mapping to the annexure scheme. For each document: Serial No., Description, Annexure Label. Only list documents mentioned in evidence. If evidence is empty, list standard expected documents with {{DOCUMENT_DESCRIPTION}} placeholders.

sections.heading: VERIFICATION
sections.body:
I, {{PLAINTIFF_NAME}}, the Plaintiff above-named, do hereby verify that the contents of the above plaint are true and correct to my knowledge, and the contents based on legal advice are believed to be true on the basis of such advice. No part of this plaint is false and nothing material has been concealed therefrom.

Verified at {{COURT_PLACE}} on this {{FILING_DATE}}.

As per Order VI Rule 15 of the Code of Civil Procedure, 1908.

SUIT FOR RECOVERY OF Rs. {{CLAIM_AMOUNT}}/- (Rupees {{CLAIM_AMOUNT_WORDS}} Only) WITH INTEREST AND COSTS
(Filed under Section 26 of the Code of Civil Procedure, 1908)

filled_sections.heading: BETWEEN
filled_sections.text: Primary Plaintiff: {{PLAINTIFF_NAME}}, aged {{PLAINTIFF_AGE}} years, {{PLAINTIFF_OCCUPATION}}, residing at {{PLAINTIFF_ADDRESS}}. Opposite Defendant: {{DEFENDANT_NAME}}, aged {{DEFENDANT_AGE}} years, {{DEFENDANT_OCCUPATION}}, residing at {{DEFENDANT_ADDRESS}}. The Plaintiff is sui juris and competent to sue and be sued.

filled_sections.heading: JURISDICTION
filled_sections.text:
The Territorial jurisdiction for this suit lies in the District Court at {{COURT_PLACE}}, {{STATE}}. The Defendant resides and carries on business at {{PLACEHOLDER_NAME}}, which is located within the territorial limits of this Court and hence within its jurisdiction. The Plaintiff paid Rs. 20,00,000 to the Defendant on {{TRANSACTION_DATE}} at {{PLACEHOLDER_NAME}} in pursuance of the agreement dated {{AGREEMENT_DATE}} executed at {{PLACEHOLDER_NAME}}. The cause of action arose at {{PLACEHOLDER_NAME}} on {{CAUSE_OF_ACTION_DATE}} when the Defendant defaulted in fulfilling the contractual obligations, and the cause of action is continuing up to the date of filing.

Pecuniary jurisdiction is satisfied as the Plaintiff seeks recovery of Rs. 20,00,000, which falls within the monetary limits of the District Court of {{STATE}} for suits of this nature. The suit prayer includes refund of the advance with interest and costs, in accordance with the facts stated and the applicable monetary jurisdiction of this Court.

filled_sections.heading: FACTS OF THE CASE
filled_sections.text:
1. The Plaintiff, {{PLAINTIFF_NAME}}, and the Defendant, {{DEFENDANT_NAME}}, had a standing business acquaintance and, on {{AGREEMENT_DATE}}, entered into a written arrangement under which the Plaintiff agreed to advance funds for a contemplated business transaction between the parties. Annexure A contains the primary transaction document evidencing the terms of this arrangement.

2. The terms of the agreement provided that the Plaintiff would advance Rs 20,00,000 to the Defendant for the purpose of the contemplated transaction, with the understanding that the Defendant would apply the funds to the transaction and would perform the agreed deliverables within the period set out in Annexure A, including issuance of an invoice and settlement of subsequent payments as contemplated by the agreement.

3. The parties’ obligations and timeline were that the Plaintiff would transfer Rs 20,00,000 to the Defendant by {{TRANSACTION_DATE}}, and the Defendant would appropriate the funds to the contemplated transaction and deliver the agreed goods/services by the timeframe set in Annexure A; the agreement contemplated that the Defendant would furnish an invoice and proceed with any further payments as required.

4. On {{TRANSACTION_DATE}}, the Plaintiff transferred Rs 20,00,000 to the Defendant by bank transfer, the transaction being evidenced by Annexure B (bank statement/receipt/UTR/delivery challan).

5. By {{DEFAULT_DATE}}, the Defendant failed to perform the obligations under the agreement; no goods or services were delivered, and no refund of the advance was made, despite the Plaintiff’s compliance with its payment obligation. The Plaintiff repeatedly sought performance and/or return of the funds but received no substantive response from the Defendant by that date. Annexures A and B establish the terms and the payment made.

6. On {{NOTICE_DATE}}, the Plaintiff caused a legal notice to be issued to the Defendant demanding repayment of Rs 20,00,000 together with interest and costs, a copy of which is annexed as Annexure C. The Defendant responded to the notice on {{NOTICE_REPLY_DATE}}, and the response is annexed as Annexure D. The Plaintiff further maintained correspondence in support of its demand, evidenced by Annexure E.

7. The cause of action accrued on {{CAUSE_OF_ACTION_DATE}} and is of continuing nature, in that the Defendant’s failure to refund the advance continues up to the filing of this suit. The suit seeks recovery of Rs 20,00,000 with interest and costs, and the relevant court fee will be calculated as per law on the suit value. The filing is intended before the District Court on {{FILING_DATE}}. The plaint is verified in compliance with the applicable procedural requirements, and the Plaintiff confirms that the facts stated herein are true to the best of its knowledge and belief; the attached Annexures A, B, C, D, and E are true copies in support of the facts stated above.

filled_sections.heading: LEGAL BASIS AND GROUNDS
filled_sections.text: The Plaintiff relies on Section 65 of the Indian Contract Act, 1872, which creates a right to recover money paid under a contract where the consideration fails entirely; the legal rule is that where money is paid in respect of an agreement and the consideration fails, the party who received the money must refund it along with such interest and costs as may be permissible. The triggering conditions are (i) money paid by the Plaintiff to the Defendant under an existing contract or for a contractual consideration, (ii) total failure of that consideration due to the Defendant’s default in performance, and (iii) the Plaintiff seeks repayment of the amount paid. In the present case, the Plaintiff, {{PLAINTIFF_NAME}}, paid Rs 20,00,000 on {{DATE_OF_PAYMENT}} as an advance for a proposed business transaction with the Defendant, {{DEFENDANT_NAME}}. The Defendant defaulted by not performing the agreed transaction, thereby causing total failure of consideration; accordingly, the Plaintiff is entitled to recover Rs 20,00,000 together with interest and costs. The action is governed by Article 47 of the Limitation Act, which provides that for money paid upon an existing consideration which afterwards fails, the period of limitation is three years and accrues on the date of the failure; the accrual date here is {{DATE_OF_FAILURE}}, and the suit is filed within three years from that date, thereby remaining within the period of limitation. In the alternative and without prejudice to the above, if the Court does not sustain the sole ground of total failure of consideration, the Defendant’s default also amounts to breach of contract entitling the Plaintiff to damages. The claim is valued at Rs 20,00,000 for the purposes of court fee and jurisdiction, and the Plaintiff will pay court fee accordingly as per the applicable District Court schedule. (Filed under Section 26 of the Code of Civil Procedure, 1908)

1. The terms of the agreement provided that the Plaintiff would advance Rs 20,00,000 to the Defendant for the purpose of the contemplated transaction, with the understanding that the Defendant would apply the funds to the transaction and would perform the agreed deliverables within the period set out in Annexure A, including issuance of an invoice and settlement of subsequent payments as contemplated by the agreement.

3. The parties’ obligations and timeline were that the Plaintiff would transfer Rs 20,00,000 to the Defendant by {{ADVANCE_PAYMENT_DATE}}, and the Defendant would appropriate the funds to the contemplated transaction and deliver the agreed goods/services by the timeframe set in Annexure A; the agreement contemplated that the Defendant would furnish an invoice and proceed with any further payments as required.

4. On {{ADVANCE_PAYMENT_DATE}}, the Plaintiff transferred Rs 20,00,000 to the Defendant by bank transfer, the transaction being evidenced by Annexure B (bank statement/receipt/UTR/delivery challan).

5. By {{DEFAULT_DATE}}, the Defendant failed to perform the obligations under the agreement; no goods or services were delivered, and no refund of the advance was made, despite the Plaintiff’s compliance with its payment obligation. 6. On {{NOTICE_DATE}}, the Plaintiff caused a legal notice to be issued to the Defendant demanding repayment of Rs 20,00,000 together with interest and costs, a copy of which is annexed as Annexure C. The Plaintiff further maintained correspondence in support of its demand, evidenced by Annexure E.

7. The cause of action accrued on {{CAUSE_OF_ACTION_DATE}} and is of continuing nature, in that the Defendant’s failure to refund the advance continues up to the filing of this suit. The suit seeks recovery of Rs 20,00,000 with interest and costs, and the relevant court fee will be calculated as per law on the suit value. Accordingly, the instant suit to recover Rs. 20,00,000 together with interest and costs is within time, since {{FILING_DATE}} falls within three years from {{DEFAULT_DATE}}.


VALUATION AND COURT FEE

The suit is valued at Rs 20,00,000 for the purposes of court fee and jurisdiction. The court fee payable on the plaint is Rs {{COURT_FEE_AMOUNT}} under the applicable State Court Fees Act.


INTEREST

The Plaintiff paid Rs. 20,00,000 to the Defendant on {{ADVANCE_PAYMENT_DATE}} in pursuance of the agreement dated {{AGREEMENT_DATE}} for a business transaction, and the Defendant failed to perform the agreed obligation by {{DEFAULT_DATE}}, resulting in a total failure of consideration. The cause of action first accrued on {{CAUSE_OF_ACTION_DATE}} and is continuing by reason of the Defendant’s ongoing wrongful retention of the said amount. In view of the commercial character of the transaction and the Defendant’s wrongful retention, the Plaintiff seeks pre-suit interest on the principal sum from {{DEFAULT_DATE}} to {{FILING_DATE}} at the rate of twelve percent per annum, as a fair reflection of the time value of money and as reasonable compensation for loss and inconvenience caused by the breach; this rate is justified by the needs of commercial certainty and aligns with prevailing bank rates for similar commercial funds. The Plaintiff further claims pendente lite interest on the same principal from {{FILING_DATE}} to the date of decree under Section 34 CPC; the rate shall be such as the Court thinks fit in view of the circumstances, with the Plaintiff submitting that the prevailing market rate for commercial lending provides a reasonable benchmark for the Court’s exercise of its discretion. Post decree, the Plaintiff seeks future interest on the decretal amount from the date of decree until realization at the rate fixed by the decree or, in the absence of a specific fixation, at the prevailing market rate, to secure full realization of the amount due. The Plaintiff thus seeks Rs. 20,00,000 with costs, together with the interest described above, to be determined by the Court in law and equity.


PRAYER

PRAYER

(a) Decree be drawn in favour of the Plaintiff for Rs. 2,000,000 (Rupees Twenty Lakhs Only) being the principal amount advanced by the Plaintiff to the Defendant on {{ADVANCE_PAYMENT_DATE}} in relation to the business transaction evidenced by the agreement dated {{AGREEMENT_DATE}}.

(b) Pre-suit interest on Rs. 2,000,000 at the rate of {{PRE_SUIT_INTEREST_RATE}} per annum accruing from {{DEFAULT_DATE}} to {{FILING_DATE}} amounting to Rs. {{PRE_SUIT_INTEREST_AMOUNT}}.

(c) Pendente lite interest under Section 34 CPC on Rs. 2,000,000 at the rate of {{PENDENTE_LITE_RATE}} per annum from {{CAUSE_OF_ACTION_DATE}} until the date of decree, amounting to Rs. {{PENDENTE_LITE_AMOUNT}}.
